blocks.statement: I can describe voltage as the ENERGY each coulomb of charge carries (J/C), use the water-tank analogy to predict whether charge will flow, and identify the voltage of common sources (AA = 1.5V, 9V battery, household = 120V).

blocks.whatWeKnow: From Unit 4: energy is conserved and can be transferred. From Day 1: charge is the fundamental quantity.
blocks.connection: Voltage marries them: how much ENERGY each unit of CHARGE carries. The 9V battery in the Car Project (Day 17 preview) carries 9 J per C.

blocks.markdown:
## Energy per coulomb

Voltage glues Unit 4 (energy) to Unit 7 (charge):

**V = U / q** — joules of energy carried PER COULOMB of charge.

A 9V battery stamps 9 J onto every coulomb that passes through it. A 1.5V AA cell: only 1.5 J/C. A wall outlet: 120 J/C. Rearranged, **U = q·V** tells you the TOTAL energy delivered when charge q flows through voltage V.

The water-tank picture: voltage is like water-tank HEIGHT. Water flows from the high tank to the low tank — and charge flows from high voltage to low voltage. No height difference, no flow; no voltage difference, no current. (Current itself is tomorrow.)

Common sources to memorize: **AA cell = 1.5V · 9V battery = 9V · car battery = 12V · US household outlet = 120V**.

terms.definition: Electric potential difference. The energy carried per coulomb of charge. V = U / q. Units: volts (V) = joules / coulomb. A 9V battery means 9 J of energy per coulomb of charge that passes through. A 1.5V AA cell: 1.5 J/C.
terms.cognate: Sp. voltaje · Pt. voltagem · HC voltaj

terms.definition: The energy a charge has due to its position in an electric field. Like gravitational PE = mgh, but for charge: U = q·V. A 0.5 C charge in a 9V battery has U = 0.5 · 9 = 4.5 J of potential energy waiting to be released through the circuit. Potential energy belongs to the CHARGE; voltage is per-coulomb.
terms.cognate: Sp. energía potencial eléctrica · Pt. energia potencial elétrica · HC enèji potansyèl elektrik

terms.definition: The voltage a battery (or other source) PROVIDES to push charge around a circuit. Confusingly NOT a force — it's a voltage. Symbol: ε. Units: volts. A fresh 9V battery has EMF ≈ 9V; as it drains, the EMF drops. The 'force' name is historical.
terms.cognate: Sp. fuerza electromotriz · Pt. força eletromotriz · HC fòs elektwomotè

blocks.title: Voltage is energy PER charge — not total energy
blocks.markdown: A 9V battery has limited TOTAL energy (depends on its size) but the same 9V no matter how full. And EMF is measured in VOLTS, not newtons — the 'force' word is misleading; it's really a per-charge ENERGY.

blocks.instruction: Two water tanks at different heights. Label which is 'high voltage' and which is 'low voltage'. Mark the JOULES PER COULOMB analogy.
prompts: Two tanks, one higher than the other.
Arrow for the flow direction (high → low).

blocks.prompt: ENERGY DELIVERED BY A BATTERY — A 9V battery pushes 0.5 C of charge through a circuit. How much energy did the battery deliver?
blocks.answer: U = 4.5 J of electrical energy delivered to the circuit.

blocks.prompt: SAME CHARGE, DIFFERENT BATTERY — The same 0.5 C of charge passes through a 1.5V AA battery. How much energy this time?
blocks.answer: U = 0.75 J. Same charge, less voltage → less energy. Six times less, because 9/1.5 = 6.

blocks.prompt: YOUR TURN — CHARGE NEEDED FOR 100 J. You want a 9V battery to deliver 100 J of energy to a circuit. How many coulombs of charge must flow through?
blocks.equationHint: Rearrange U = q·V to solve for q. Expect a number bigger than 10.

blocks.title: Why so many small batteries?
blocks.markdown: AA is 1.5V — too low for most uses. So we stack them in series: 4 AA = 6V, 6 AA = 9V. **Voltages in series ADD.** The Car Project uses a single 9V (compact rectangular) for simplicity.

blocks.prompt: How much energy does a 12V car battery deliver when 1.0 C of charge flows through it? Show one line of work. Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: Voltage measures ___ per ___. A 9V battery gives ___ J to every coulomb of charge that flows through. To deliver more total energy, you need more ___ (charge to flow).

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **33.4–33.7** (electrical potential energy, electric potential (voltage), energy storage, and the Van de Graaff generator). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.

blocks.title: Electric Fields and Potential — read & practice
